revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:円筒埴輪]]
[[File:Kofun, haniwa cilindrico, IV-V sec.JPG|thumb|200px|Cylindrical Haniwa (円筒埴輪)]] '''Cylindrical Haniwa''' (円筒埴輪) are a type of {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} erected on top of {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. These haniwa are characterized by their {{ill|clay pipe|ja|土管|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay pipe|qq=}}-like cylindrical shape and are among the earliest haniwa to appear. Another distinct type of haniwa is the {{ill|Representational Haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Representational Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}. == Overview == The origins of cylindrical haniwa are traced to the {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi period|1=Yayoi period}} late phase (2nd century CE), particularly in the {{ill|Kibi|en|Kibi|qid=Q404065|lt=Kibi region|1=Kibi}} centered around present-day {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|qid=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}. These are thought to have developed from {{ill|special ceremonial pottery|ja|特殊器台・特殊壺|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|1=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|lt=Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/special ceremonial pottery|13=User:Immanuelle/Ceremonial vessel stands or jars|qq=}} (also known as special pedestal-shaped or jar-shaped pottery) used in {{ill|Funeral|en|Funeral|qid=Q201676|lt=funerary rites|1=Funeral}} of {{ill|Yayoi Kofuns|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi Kofuns|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}, such as the {{ill|Tatezuka Kofun|ja|楯築墳丘墓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatezuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 楯築遺跡|1=Tatetsuki Site|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11541408}}. By the mid-3rd century, during the funeral ceremonies of the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|qid=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}}, "Miyayama-style ceremonial pedestals and jars" emerged, which eventually gave rise to the earliest style of cylindrical haniwa, the "Tsuzuki-style cylindrical haniwa." Cylindrical haniwa became widely used, though somewhat later than the spread of {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}, and serve as important chronological markers for dating kofun. === Research History === Research into cylindrical haniwa dates back to the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}}, with discussions initiated alongside the advent of modern {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|qid=Q23498|lt=archaeology|1=Archaeology}}. Between 1888 and 1901, {{ill|Tsuboi Shōgorō|ja|坪井正五郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuboi Shōgorō|1=Tsuboi Shōgorō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11425705}} studied cylindrical haniwa and proposed the theory that their numerous brush-stroke patterns (known as ''hakeme'') imitated wooden fences (''shibagaki'') used as {{ill|retaining wall|ja|土留|lt=retaining walls|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/retaining wall|1=retaining wall|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17232614}} to prevent mound erosion. This theory sparked debates with scholars such as {{ill|Wada Senkichi|ja|和田千吉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wada Senkichi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and Mitsui Seizaburō.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Tsuboi|first=Shōgorō|year=1888|title=Reconstruction of Ancient Customs Based on Haniwa|journal=Tokyo Anthropological Journal|volume=3|pages=100–108|qq=}}</ref> In 1967, {{ill|Kondō Yoshirō|ja|近藤義郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kondō Yoshirō|1=Yoshirō Kondō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11638900}} and {{ill|Harunari Hideshō|ja|春成秀爾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harunari Hideshō|1=Hideji Harunari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11513502}} demonstrated that cylindrical haniwa originated from the special ceremonial pottery of the Kibi region during the late Yayoi period and evolved into their final form by the late 3rd century.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kondō|first=Yoshirō|last2=Harunari|first2=Hideshō|year=1967|title=The Origins of Haniwa|journal=Kōkogaku Kenkyū|volume=13|pages=13–35|qq=}}</ref> In 1978, Kawanishi Hiroyuki published "A Comprehensive Study of Cylindrical Haniwa," categorizing their attributes, such as the arrangement of bands (''taga'') and brush strokes (''hakeme''). His classification contributed to chronological dating standards for kofun.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Kawanishi|first=Hiroyuki|year=1978|title=Comprehensive Study of Cylindrical Haniwa|journal=Kōkogaku Zasshi|volume=64|pages=95–164|qq=}}</ref> == Types, Sizes, and Features == Originally used to support jars, cylindrical haniwa evolved into structures arranged in fence-like rows. The main types include: * '''Ordinary Cylindrical Haniwa''': Simple earthenware tubes. * '''Morning Glory-Shaped Cylindrical Haniwa''': Featuring a flared trumpet-like upper section. * '''Fin-Attached Cylindrical Haniwa''': Equipped with vertical fin-like protrusions on their sides. The cylindrical haniwa often have brush-stroke patterns (''hakeme'') left by tools used to smooth their surfaces. Their size ranges from several dozen centimeters to about two meters, with the largest example, found at the {{ill|Mesuriyama Kofun|en|Mesuriyama Kofun|qid=Q11343632|lt=Mesuriyama Kofun|1=Mesuriyama Kofun}} in {{ill|Sakurai, Nara|en|Sakurai, Nara|qid=Q127026|lt=Sakurai|1=Sakurai, Nara}}, measuring 2.4 meters. == Gallery == <gallery> File:特殊器台・特殊壺 (複製).JPG|Replica of special ceremonial pottery from the Yayoi period ({{ill|National Museum of Japanese History|en|National Museum of Japanese History|qid=Q3330690|lt=National Museum of Japanese History|1=National Museum of Japanese History}}) File:Periodo kofun, haniwa, cilindri, VI sec.JPG|Ordinary and morning glory-shaped haniwa ({{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}) File:メスリ山古墳出土 大型円筒埴輪.JPG|Largest cylindrical haniwa (2.4 meters) from {{ill|Mesuriyama Kofun|en|Mesuriyama Kofun|qid=Q11343632|lt=Mesuriyama Kofun|1=Mesuriyama Kofun}} ({{ill|Nara Prefecture Archaeological Research Institute|ja|奈良県立橿原考古学研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara Prefecture Archaeological Research Institute|qq=}}) File:Hodota-kofungun Hachimanzuka-kofun-2.JPG|Cylindrical and morning glory-shaped haniwa at {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Cluster|1=Hodota Kofun Cluster}} ({{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|qid=Q336438|lt=Takasaki|1=Takasaki}}) </gallery> == References == {{Reflist|2|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} * {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}} * {{ill|Figural haniwa|ja|形象埴輪|1=Representational Haniwa|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Figural haniwa|13=User:Immanuelle/Representational Haniwa|qq=}}
{{Kofun navbar|qq=}}
